top_categories.workout_context: At mile 60 of 106, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
Cinturón Ciclista a Mallorca's mixed terrain rewards steady threshold power. Surging and recovering costs more watts than just holding.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Cinturón Ciclista a Mallorca's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before February.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Cinturón Ciclista a Mallorca. Train it until it feels boring.
When someone attacks on mixed terrain, you have 10 seconds to respond or you're off the back. This builds that response.
106 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 106 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Long rides with hard efforts mixed in. Simulates what Cinturón Ciclista a Mallorca's mixed terrain actually does to you.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 106 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.
Tempo is Cinturón Ciclista a Mallorca's race pace. The wattage you'll hold for 106 miles on mixed terrain. Train it until it's automatic.
Cinturón Ciclista a Mallorca's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 106 miles.
At Cinturón Ciclista a Mallorca, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Cinturón Ciclista a Mallorca’s 106 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, TT Threshold, and Race Simulation to prepare you for 106 miles of mixed terrain in Mallorca, Spain.
